coredata.dc:title: A fast, low-cost, sensitive, selective, and non-laborious method based on functionalized magnetic nanoparticles, magnetic solid-phase extraction, and fluorescent carbon dots for the fluorimetric determination of copper in wines without prior sample treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Costa, Matheus H.
Ferreira, Danilo T.S.
Pádua, Jonathan E.S.
Fernandes, Julys P.A.
Santos, Josué Carinhanha C.
Cunha, Francisco Antônio S.
Araujo, Mario Cesar Ugulino
coredata.dc:description: A new fluorimetric method for copper(II) determination in wines was developed combining functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (FMNP) and fluorescent carbon dots (FCD). To produce FMNP, Fe3O4 was coated with Al2O3 forming Fe3O4@Al2O3 core–shell magnetic nanoparticles and functionalized with PAN and SDS. FCD was synthesized from pineapple juice through hydrothermal carbonization. For copper determination, aliquots of wine, the FMNP dispersion, and Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 4.0) were mixed under stirring to allow the adsorption of copper by FMNP. Cu-FMNP complex was attracted by a niobium magnet and, after discarding the non-magnetic material, the copper(II) ions were eluted with an FCD dispersion before fluorescence quenching measurements. The proposed method presented a linear range from 0.020 to 0.100 mg L−1 (r2 = 0.9953), RSD (intraday) < 3.0%, and recovery rates from 96 to 105 %. FMNP and FCD properties permitted extraction/preconcentration/determination of copper within 1 min with an enrichment factor of nine and without prior sample treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic nanoparticles
Magnetic solid-phase extraction
Analytical sample pretreatment
Fluorescent carbon dots
Red wines
Wine analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis, coating, and functionalization of the FMNP
The stability, shelf-life of the reagents, and waste disposal of the chemicals

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the proposed method.
SEM images and XRD patterns of the bare Fe3O4 (a, d) and the Fe3O4@Al2O3 core–shell MNPs (b, e). The size distribution of the bare Fe3O4 (c). XRD patterns of the FCD (f).

Fourier transform-infrared (a) and UV–Vis absorption and excitation and emission fluorescence (b) spectra of the fluorescent carbon dots (FCD). Emission fluorescence spectra of bare FCD with increasingly longer excitation wavelengths (c). Emission fluorescence spectra of bare FCD with increasing pH (d). Diameters of Cu-FCD complexes and bare FCD (e). Emission fluorescence spectra of Cu-FCD complexes with increasing copper concentrations (f).

Effect on the fluorescence quenching of Cu-FCD complex of FMNP contents (0.6, 1.2, and 1.8 mg mL−1) in ethanolic dispersion, stirring times 1.0 (a), 2.0 (b) and 3.0 min (c), volumes (1.0, 3.0 and 5.0 mL) of 0.6 mg mL−1 FMNP dispersion (d) and volumes (50–300 µL) of FCD dispersion (e) and times (10–60 s) of elution (f).
Results and recovery values for copper (II) determination in wine samples.
Comparison of proposed with other methods for copper (II) determination in wine samples.

row.entry: Concentration of copper in wines (mg L–1)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ETAAS: Electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry; ICP-OES: Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry; USN-ICP-OES: Ultrasonic nebulization coupled to (USN-ICP-OES); TXRF: Total Reflection X-ray Fluorescence; SEC-ICP-MS: Size exclusion chromatography (SEC) hyphenated to Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS); LOD: Limit of detection; NM: Not mentioned.

head.ce:title: A fast, low-cost, sensitive, selective, and non-laborious method based on functionalized magnetic nanoparticles, magnetic solid-phase extraction, and fluorescent carbon dots for the fluorimetric determination of copper in wines without prior sample treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Universidade Federal da Paraíba, Departamento de Química, P.O. Box 5093, Zip Code 58051-970, João Pessoa, Paraíba, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Universidade Federal da Paraíba, Departamento de Química, P.O. Box 5093, Zip Code 58051-970, João Pessoa, Paraíba, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Universidade Federal de Alagoas, Instituto de Química e Biotecnologia, Campus A.C. Simões, Tabuleiro dos Martins, Zip Code 57072-900, Maceió, Alagoas, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Campus A.C. Simões, Tabuleiro dos Martins, Zip Code 57072-900

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Universidade Federal de Alagoas, Instituto de Química e Biotecnologia, Campus A.C. Simões, Tabuleiro dos Martins, Zip Code 57072-900, Maceió, Alagoas, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new fluorimetric method for copper(II) determination in wines was developed combining functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (FMNP) and fluorescent carbon dots (FCD). To produce FMNP, Fe3O4 was coated with Al2O3 forming Fe3O4@Al2O3 core–shell magnetic nanoparticles and functionalized with PAN and SDS. FCD was synthesized from pineapple juice through hydrothermal carbonization. For copper determination, aliquots of wine, the FMNP dispersion, and Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 4.0) were mixed under stirring to allow the adsorption of copper by FMNP. Cu-FMNP complex was attracted by a niobium magnet and, after discarding the non-magnetic material, the copper(II) ions were eluted with an FCD dispersion before fluorescence quenching measurements. The proposed method presented a linear range from 0.020 to 0.100 mg L−1 (r2 = 0.9953), RSD (intraday) < 3.0%, and recovery rates from 96 to 105 %. FMNP and FCD properties permitted extraction/preconcentration/determination of copper within 1 min with an enrichment factor of nine and without prior sample treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Until recently, the determination of copper content was essential for monitoring wine quality and evaluating the winemaking process (Sauvage, Frank, Stearne, & Millikan, 2002). High copper concentrations in wines led to oxidative reactions or the formation of precipitates, affecting wine organoleptic characteristics such as aroma, taste, and color, causing turbidity alterations and browning (Grindlay, Mora, Gras, & de Loos-Vollebregt, 2011). These problems occurred mainly during the winemaking process, fermentation, maturation, and storage of wines. They are of concern to winemakers because, for humans, a high intake of copper (>1.0 mg L–1) leads to the formation of reactive oxygen species and imbalance cellular metabolism (Ibanez, Carreon-Alvarez, Barcena-Soto, & Casillas, 2008).

More recently, according to Sun et al. (2018), the location of the vineyard, the variety of grapes, and the aging process are all significant in determining copper content in grapes and wine. Their research indicates that copper rarely degrades or moves in arable soil layers, which causes copper enrichment in vineyard soils. As a result, given the long-term use of copper fungicides in viticulture, there has occurred considerable copper accumulation in vineyard soils. Sun et al. (2018) also stated that the copper levels in the vineyard soils, grapes, and wines were correlated; the copper contents ranging from 0.00052 to 0.663 mg L–1, representing approximately one percent the level found in grapes and one ten-thousandth that found in soils. In addition, these authors found that red wines showed a significantly higher copper content than white wines, while in the red/white grape and soil samples, no significant differences were observed.

High residual copper can contribute to an increased rate of oxidative deterioration, which results in the wine being darkened (Pyrzyńska, 2004). It is worth mentioning that polyphenols can chelate divalent cations such as copper. Wines produced from Cabernet Sauvignon are a rich source of those compounds (Lisov et al., 2020). However. excessive residual concentrations of copper in wine are typically due to inappropriate high additions of copper during the so-called copper fining of reduced volatile sulfides. Because of this, copper additions and residual copper concentrations in wine are set by various regulatory agencies and vary by country: OIV and USA limits for copper in wines are 1.0 mg L−1 (OIV – Oenology resolutions, 2011; TTBGov — US Treating Materials., 2020).

The development of new analytical methods is important to determine the concentration of copper established by the various regulatory agencies (<1.0 mg L–1). For this, numerous instrumental methods have been reported in the literature for example electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry, ETAAS (Alkış et al., 2014); inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry, ICP–OES (Moreno et al., 2008); ultrasonic nebulization(UNS) coupled to ICP-OES, USN-ICP-OES (Lara, Cerutti, Salonia, Olsina, & Martinez, 2005); total reflection X-ray fluorescence, TXRF (Pessanha, Carvalho, Becker, & von Bohlen, 2010), and size exclusion chromatography (SEC) hyphenated to inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Latorre et al., 2019).

These analytical methods require expensive instrumentations for acquisition, operation, and maintenance and use laborious and time-consuming sample pretreatment, such as SEC, wet digestion, drying, and ashing. Moreover, these sample pretreatments present risks of contamination and analyte loss. To overcome these drawbacks, Milovanovic et al. (2019) developed a new method for wine analysis that uses biosensors for the classification of wines according to the analysis of carboxylic acid levels, without prior sample preparation. However, analytical methods for copper determination using inexpensive instrumentation without prior sample pretreatment have not been found in the literature.

Wine is a complex matrix of water, alcohol, sugars, and other inorganic and organic compounds. An alternative for determining copper or other metals without sample pretreatment is the use of ethanol-containing standards or the standard-addition method (SAM) to minimize physical or chemical interferences (Pohl, 2007). The analysis of alcoholic beverages through matrix-matching calibration or SAM, however, often leads to inaccurate results because of possible matrix effects (Pohl, 2007). Moreover, the SAM is a slow and laborious process that generates large amounts of waste (Benı́tez, Castro, & Barroso, 2002). To overcome these drawbacks, a good alternative for determining metals in complex matrices as wines is the development of a new analytical method that combines the use of functionalized magnetic nanoparticles-FMNP (Cunha et al., 2018), magnetic solid-phase extraction – MSPE (Andrade et al., 2019; Barreto et al., 2018) and fluorescent carbon dots - FCD (Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2014).

In recent years, nanotechnology has been widely used in numerous applications, such as for the synthesis of new materials, miniaturization of electrical components, treatment and/or diagnosis of diseases, renewable energy, and textile manufacturing (Hernández-Hernández, Álvarez-Romero, Contreras-López, Aguilar-Arteaga, & Castañeda-Ovando, 2017). Nanoparticle-assisted methods for sample preparation and analysis, especially those based on functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (FMNP) (Cunha et al., 2018) and fluorescent carbon dots (FCD) (Lim et al., 2014), have been employed to develop more straightforward, more cost-effective, and greener on-site assays (Bendicho, Bendicho-Lavilla, & Lavilla, 2016). Among them, magnetic solid-phase extraction (MSPE) has many advantages including simple operation, short extraction time, low organic solvent consumption, and easy automation, consequently improving analysis speed as well as reducing costs and waste generation (Bendicho et al., 2016).

In MSPE, the FMNP is added to the sample matrix so that the analyte can be adsorbed on their surface. Then, the system adsorbent-analyte is separated from the solution by using an external magnetic field. After its elution, the analyte is analyzed (Pan, Mao, Chen, & Wang, 2013). Since the FMNP retain no residual magnetism after the field removal, they can be reused or recycled, simplifying sample pretreatment without requiring filtration or centrifugation (Faraji et al., 2010).

Fluorescent carbon dots (FCD) have attracted considerable research interest due to their excellent biocompatibility, low-cost preparation, and potential applications for metal quantification. These fast and highly sensitive nanoparticles are promising as probes mainly due to their photostability and strong fluorescence intensity, which are proportional to metal ion concentration (Esteves da Silva & Gonçalves, 2011). However, selectivity issues and the difficulty of detecting analytes in complex chemical environments constitute some limitations on the direct use of FCD (Qu, Wei, & Sun, 2018).

This study aimed to develop a fast, inexpensive, sensitive, selective, and non-laborious method using FMNP, MSPE, FCD, and fluorimetric detection to determine low concentrations of copper in wines (<1.0 mg L–1) without sample pretreatment. To produce FMNP, initially, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) were synthetized utilizing the chemical co-precipitation process. After, the MNPs were coated with Al2O3 forming Fe3O4@Al2O3 core–shell MNPs and functionalized with PAN and SDS (Shemirani & Akhavi, 2001) to ensure that the formed FMNP would be selective for copper. As well as being small in size and having superparamagnetic properties (Barreto et al., 2018), the FMNP did not influence copper detection by fluorescence, so this was used for the magnetic extraction of trace-levels of copper from wine samples, forming Cu-FMNP complex. FCD were synthesized from pineapple juice through hydrothermal carbonization. Elution of the Cu-FMNP complex with a solution containing FCD yields the fluorescence Cu-FCD complex, which enables the fluorimetric determination of the extracted copper from wines (Qu et al., 2018), further decreasing analysis costs. The proposed method was statistically validated against a reference procedure and its accuracy was assessed through spiked recovery tests (Cunha et al., 2018).

All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade. Deionized water (Milli-Q Millipore system) was used for the preparation of all solutions. The working solutions were prepared daily in 2.0% (v/v) HNO3 from stock solutions of 1000 mg L−1 Cu(II) (Titrisol, Merck, USA) and 1000 mg L−1 Pd2+ (Sigma-Aldrich, USA).

To produce FMNP, the following reagents were used: ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), aluminum isopropoxide, ethanol, 1-(2-pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), NaOH, HNO3 and HCl. All were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA, and used without previous purification. A working solution of 0.25% (m/v) PAN in ethanol (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was used in this synthesis. To determine the quantum yield, quinine sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was used as the fluorescence standard.

For the pH study, a stock Britton-Robinson buffer (BRB) solution (pH = 2) was prepared by dissolving 2.7 mL of H3PO4, 2.3 mL of acetic acid, and 5.0 g of H3BO3 (all reagents from Merck, USA) in a liter of water. BRB solutions with pH ranging from 4 to 8 were prepared using 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH (Sigma-Aldrich, USA).
To reduce environmental impacts, all residues generated in this work were treated according to protocols and recommendations suggested in the literature (Nascimento & Tenuta Fillho, 2010).

The analyzed red wine samples (Syrah and Cabernet Sauvignon) were acquired from São Francisco Valley, Pernambuco State, Brazil. This wine producer is located between 8° and 9° latitude of the South Hemisphere (zone tropical) in the Northeast of Brazil. These Brazilian tropical wines are elaborated only using grapes of Vitis vinifera L. cultivars which are cultivated in soils (as Latosols) with variations in physical and chemical characteristics. To prevent an undesirable content of copper in wines, the Brazilian winemaker follows the withdrawal period of chemicals before wine production (Pereira, 2011).

Fluorescence measurements were performed with a spectrofluorometer (Agilent, G9800A) using excitation and emission slits of 10 nm and a quartz fluorometric cell (10 × 10 mm). An AA 6800 atomic absorption spectrometer (Shimadzu,) equipped with an autosampler (ASC-6100) was used for the reference method. The monochromator bandwidth of the absorption spectrometer was 0.7 mm. Peak areas of absorbance were measured at 324.8 nm. Argon 99.999% (White Martins) was used as a purge gas with a flow rate of 250 mL min−1 during all stages except atomization, in which the gas flow was interrupted. A muffle oven (EDG, 7000) was used for synthesizing the FCD. A compact centrifuge (Hermle, Z206A) with a maximum rotational speed of 5000 rpm was used for the separation of particulate material. A rotavapor (IKA) was used for the vacuum drying of the synthesized nanomaterial. To measure and control the pH of the samples, a pH meter (Metrohm, 713) was used. A scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, DSM-940 A) was used to characterize the morphology and size distribution of the FMNP. A diffractometer (XRD-6000, Shimadzu) was used to determine the crystallographic characteristics of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@Al2O3 core–shell microspheres. To characterize the functional groups on the surface of the FCD, a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Applied Instrument Technologies, Diamond 20) and a diffractometer was used. The diameters of FCD and Cu-FCD complex were measured using dynamic light scattering (DLS) technology (Microtrac Zetatrac, NPA 151-31A 10 M).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, coating, and functionalization of the FMNP
ce:section.ce:para: The FMNP were synthesized utilizing the chemical co-precipitation process, coated with alumina, and functionalized with SDS and PAN according to previously reported methods (Andrade et al., 2019; Barreto et al., 2018; Cunha et al., 2018; Shemirani & Akhavi, 2001).

Initially, 5.2 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 2 g of FeCl2 4H2O were dissolved in 850 µL of concentrated HCl and 25 mL of deoxygenated water. The resulting solution was added dropwise to 250 mL of a 1.5 mol L−1 NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. After that, the pH value was adjusted to 11–12 and the solution was bubbled with nitrogen gas for 2 h, even after the formation of a black precipitate (Fe3O4 nanoparticles). Then, the suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm three times and was kept under stirring. A volume of 500 mL of a 10 mmol L−1 HCl solution was added to the suspension to neutralize anionic charges on the surface of the magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs). The suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm again and peptized with water resulting in the formation of a clear, transparent cationic colloid (hydrosol) of Fe3O4 MNPs, which was finally dried in an oven at 60 °C and stored.

The coating of the dried Fe3O4 MNPs was carried out as follows: 0.5 g aluminum isopropoxide was dissolved in 50.0 mL of ethanol. After that, 100 mg of Fe3O4 MNPs was dispersed in this ethanolic solution by sonication for 5 min using a bath sonicator. A solution of 1:5 (v/v) water/ethanol was added dropwise to the suspension of Fe3O4@Al2O3 MNPs under vigorous magnetic stirring for approximately 15 min. The suspension was then stirred for 1 h before separation and washing with ethanol. After five cycles of separation/washing/redispersion with ethanol, the powder Fe3O4@Al2O3 core–shell MNPs were obtained by drying in an oven and by calcining in nitrogen (500 °C, 1 h).

The functionalization was carried out as follows: first, 1.5 g of Fe3O4@Al2O3 core–shell MNPs, 100 mg of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), and 1 mL of 0.25% (m/v) PAN in ethanol were mixed, and ethanol was added until the dispersion had a final volume of 100 mL. The pH was adjusted to approximately 1.0 with a 3.0 mol L−1 HCl solution. After, the mixture was kept under stirring (200 rpm, 15 min). Finally, the FMNP were separated with the aid of an external magnetic field and, after the supernatant was discarded, washed with 50 mL of ethanol three times. Finally, FMNP were dried in an oven at 60 °C and stored.

FCD were synthesized from pineapple juice by using a green hydrothermal procedure as described elsewhere (De & Karak, 2013). Initially, pineapple juice was prepared by mixing 100.0 g of peeled pineapple in 100.0 mL of deionized water and solid content removed with the help of a slow filtration by the paper filter (Quanty® JP42 – blue band). Then, 30 mL of ethanol (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was mixed with 50 mL of the filtrate and the resulting mixture was autoclaved (160 °C, 6 h). After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, then 50.0 mL ethanol was added to the mixture which was then centrifugated (5000 rpm, 20 min) to remove larger and undesirable particles. The synthesized FCD were dialyzed in deionized water with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Dalton (Sigma, PURG10015 dialysis kit) under constant stirring over 72 h to remove non-fluorescent material. Finally, the viscous material was dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water and stocked. This resulting FCD dispersion was used for the characterization and quantum yield determination of the FCD, as well as, in the proposed method.

The quantum yield of the FCD was determined according to Eq. (1) below:
(1)

∅ R

FCDS

FCDs
, I FCD,

FCD
,

FCDs
,
∅ R
, I R,
η R
, and
A R
are respectively the quantum yield, the fluorescence intensity, the refractive index, and the absorbance of FCD and the 1.25x10−4 mol L−1 quinine sulfate solution in 0.100 mol L−1 sulphuric acid, used as fluorescence standard reference (
∅ = 54
).

The overall analytical procedure of the proposed fluorimetric method is shown in Fig. 1 . Initially, 1.00 mL of the FMNP dispersion in ethanol (0.6 mg L−1) and 1.00 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer (BRB) solution (pH = 4) were added to 20.0 mL of the wine sample and mixed under stirring for 60 s to allow the adsorption of copper by FMNP. A niobium magnet was then placed close to the vessel for 3 s, attracting the Cu-FMNP complex. After the non-magnetic material was discarded, the copper(II) ions were eluted from the Cu-FMNP complex with 200.0 μL of the FCD dispersion, and 1.80 mL of deionized water was added to the resultant mixture. Finally, the fluorescence quenching of
Cu-FCD was measured in the spectrofluorometer using excitation at 370 nm and emission at 460 nm.

A similar matrix-matching method based on that described by Caldas, Raposo, Gomes Neto, and Barbosa (2009) and adopted as a reference method by Andrade et al. (2019) was modified to serve as a comparison method for copper determination in wine samples. The comparison method here adopted also used the graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometric (GF-AAS) technique, however, the dilution factor employed for all wines was only 1:50 (v/v). According to Grindlay et al. (2011), the matrix effect is almost eliminated when a 1:5 dilution factor is employed for elemental analysis of wines using inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES). Before the GF-AAS, measurements were taken, ethanol was added to all standard solutions and diluted samples to yield an ethanol concentration of 0.3% (v/v). All GF-AAS parameters, the concentrations, and volumes of standards, samples, and the Pd(NO3)2 modifier as well as, the construction of the analytical curve were identical to those used by Andrade et al. (2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability, shelf-life of the reagents, and waste disposal of the chemicals
ce:section.ce:para: All chemicals used in this work were recently purchased on the market and have great stability and shelf-life, facilitating their use in chemical analysis. The prepared nanomaterials (FMNP and FCD) have long-term chemical stability and excellent performance under extreme pH and temperature conditions as well as the use of organic solvents (Esteves da Silva & Gonçalves, 2011; Lucena, Simonet, Cárdenas, & Valcárcel, 2011). These characteristics allow the nanomaterials used in this work to be used in routine analyses for extended periods. It is worth highlighting that our research laboratory has a strict disposal program for all used chemicals.

The bare Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@Al2O3 core–shell MNPs were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. Before the SEM measurements were made, the MNPs were coated with a 40 nm thick gold layer to improve image resolution. As can be seen in the SEM images (Fig. 2 a and 2b), although there are some aggregates formed, both MNPs have a spherical morphology and are evenly dispersed. Similar behavior has been previously observed elsewhere (Andrade et al., 2019; Barreto et al., 2018; Cunha et al., 2018). The average size of the nanoparticles, which were measured using dynamic light scattering (DLS) technology was found to be 14.97 nm (Fig. 2c), and this size is in line with the finding elsewhere (Arévalo, Isasi, Caballero, Marco, & Martín-Hernández, 2017).

The XRD patterns of the bare Fe3O4 MNPs (Fig. 2d) and Fe3O4@Al2O3 core–shell (Fig. 2e) MNPs show characteristic Fe3O4 peaks in the range from 20° to 67°, indicating that no competitive phases were formed during the synthesis. The position and intensity of these peaks are consistent with those previously described elsewhere (Barreto et al., 2018; Cunha et al., 2018) and with the crystallographic database in the Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) files No. 39-1346 and 29-0063. Additional Fe3O4@Al2O3 MNPs peaks are attributed to alumina.

The XRD patterns of the FCD (Fig. 2f) present two sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 10° and 2θ = 30° and a small diffraction peak at 2θ = 40° for carbon (Jeong et al., 2014), suggesting an amorphous structure. The presence of more oxygen-containing groups and carbon atoms acting as alkyl chains densely packed with graphite carbons can explain this finding (Asha Jhonsi & Thulasi, 2016).

The Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the synthesized FCD is shown in Fig. 3 a. According to Tian et al. (2009) and Asha Jhonsi and Thulasi (2016), peaks at FT-IR spectrum may be attributed to stretching vibrations of COC (1255 and 1129 cm−1), –COO− (1735 cm−1), –OH, (3435 cm−1), CH3 (2925 cm−1), CH2 (2852 cm−1) and CC (1625 cm−1) groups. Moreover, as also mentioned by Asha Jhonsi and Thulasi (2016), the presence of oxygen-rich groups (carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl) on the surface of the FCD indicated that unsaturated carbons were formed during the synthesis of these nanoparticles, making them water dispersed (Kasibabu, Dsouza, Jha, & Kailasa, 2015).

The UV–Vis absorption and excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of the synthesized FCD are displayed in Fig. 3b. The peak at 260 nm and the shoulder around 320 nm at the UV–Vis absorption spectrum may be attributed to π → π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds (D. Pan, Zhang, Li, & Wu, 2010) and n → π* transitions of CO (Eda et al., 2010), respectively. Excitation and emission fluorescence peaks of FCD occur around 370 and 460 nm, respectively. These peaks may be explained by their surface homogeneity and the precursor utilized (Chu, Lee, Chang, & Liu, 2019). Emission fluorescence spectra of FCD with increasingly longer excitation wavelengths are presented in Fig. 3c.

According to Vandarkuzhali et al. (2018), the red-shift and the intensity decrease of the peaks occur due to different surface sites and particle sizes. The excitation quantum yield was found to be 4.12% at 370 nm using quinine sulfate as a reference, which is in line with the literature (De & Karak, 2013). However, the fact that these nanoparticles were synthesized from a cheap and widely available precursor may compensate for this low quantum efficiency. The pH did not have a significant impact on the intensity of emission of the FCD, as shown in Fig. 3d.
As Fig. 3e shows, the bare FCD diameter measured using DLS technology is 4.2 nm in the absence of copper, and diameters of Cu-FCD complexes increase as more copper is adsorbed in the surface of FCD.

When FCD are excited at wavelength 370 nm, the quenching process causes a sharp decrease in fluorescence intensity of FCD at 460 nm (Fig. 3f) resulting from the increase in the concentration of copper. This occurs due to the binding of the copper to the surface of the FCD, forming the Cu-FCD complex.

The Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 4.0) was chosen for the copper extraction of the wine samples because the pH 4 favors the reaction between copper and PAN (Cunha et al., 2018) in the Cu-FMNP complex formation. It is worth mentioning that when chelation between copper and PAN occurs, two five-membered chelate rings are formed. The heterocyclic nitrogen, hydroxy group, and nitrogen atom that are farthest from the pyridine ring are involved in chelate formation (Ooi, Carter, & Fernando, 1967). Moreover, tests showed that buffering the solutions did not affect the elution and fluorescence intensity measurement.

To determine the optimum content of FMNP in ethanolic dispersion for the copper extraction of the wine samples, tests were performed using 1.00 mL of the Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 4.0), 20 mL of the wine sample 6, 200 μL of the FCD solution, 1.80 of H2O, and, 0.6, 1.2, and 1.8 mg mL−1 of FMNP in ethanolic dispersion. In this test, the stirring time was also changed from 1 to 3 min. As can be seen in Fig. 4 a to 4c, an FMNP dispersion of 0.6 mg mL−1 and a stirring time of 1.0 min resulted in the highest fluorescence quenching of Cu-FCD.

Different volumes of the 0.6 mg mL−1 FMNP (Fig. 4d) and FCD (Fig. 4e) dispersions, as well as, elution times (Fig. 4f) were also tested. The highest fluorescence quenching of Cu-FCD was obtained using 1.0 mL of 0.6 mg mL−1 FMNP and 200 µL FCD and an elution time of 30 s.

To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method for copper, potential interfering substances, including Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, SO4 2–, PO4 3− and NO3− in concentrations ranging from 50.0 to 500.0 μg L–1 were added to wine samples. A difference of ± 5% between copper concentrations in the samples with and without interfering species was taken as the criterion for identifying interference (Andrade et al., 2019). Although wine is a complex matrix, no significant interference was found within this studied concentration range (Mehdinia, Jebeliyan, Kayyal, & Jabbari, 2017), indicating that the proposed method is selective for the copper determination under optimized experimental conditions.

No interactions between the FCD and other wine components, such as aromatic amino acids (tyrosine, phenylalanine, and tryptophan residues in proteins), vitamins, cofactors, and phenolic compounds (Sikorska, 2019) were found. In addition, as Mehdinia et al. (2017) and Andrade et al. (2019) methods, the proposed methodology employs a magnetic solid-phase extraction step with FMNP. All possible interferents such as high free SO2, high ethanol (up to 14.5% by volume), high acid (wine pH range 3.0 to 4.0), and high metal/mineral content (potassium, iron) are then eliminated before the detection step employing FCD.

An analytical curve was constructed from solutions of copper(II) ions prepared in triplicate in the range from 0.020 to 0.100 mg L−1. There is a good semilogarithmic correlation between the fluorescence quenching (Dong et al., 2012) and the concentration of copper, according to the estimated regression equation: log(F0/F) = (213.87 ± 0.0100) C + (349.84 ± 0.0100), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of FCD in the absence and presence of copper respectively, and C is the concentration of copper(II) in mg L–1.

To evaluate the fit for a linear model, an analysis of variance (ANOVA) was conducted using three genuine repeat measurements performed at each level of concentration. No lack of fit evidence for the linear model was observed, and the linear regression was significant at the 95% confidence level. The correlation coefficient (R2) was found to be 0.9953 and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.0082 mg L–1.

Six wine samples were analyzed in triplicate using the proposed and reference (ETAAS) methods and the results are presented in Table 1 . Because the paired t-test at a 95% confidence level indicated no significant differences among these results, the proposed method can be used to quantify copper in wine samples under optimized conditions. Also, the concentrations of copper found in this study agree with the literature (Alkış et al., 2014) and are within the limits established by the International Organization of Wine and Vine (OIV, 2020).

The relative standard deviations (RSD) for three genuine repeat sample interday and intraday analyses were<1.5% and 3.0%, respectively. Also, an enrichment factor of nine was obtained for a sample volume of 20.0 mL.

The accuracy of the proposed method was evaluated by spiking wine samples with three different known concentrations of copper (0.20, 0.50, and 0.90 mg L–1). According to Table 1, the recovery values were found to be in the range from 96 to 105% with a standard deviation of<3.1%, which indicated good reliability of the proposed method. These results show that the FCD with good fluorescence quenching have very promising applications in the detection of copper.

Table 2 compares the analytical features of the proposed method with those reported in other studies (Alkış et al., 2014; Lara et al., 2005; Latorre et al., 2019; Moreno et al., 2008; Pessanha et al., 2010). Regarding detection power, the proposed method was able to achieve a low LOD value (8.20 ng L–1) without the need to employ highly-priced instruments such as ICP-MS, ETAAS, ICP-OES, USN-ICP-OES: USN-ICP-OES, SEC-ICP-MS, and TXRF.

A new method for determining copper(II) in wine samples was successfully developed by combining the advantages of FMNP, MS and FCD. The core–shell Fe3O4@Al2O3 nanoparticles functionalized with PAN and SDS had a high surface-area-to-volume ratio, favoring the complexation reaction with copper using only 1.0 mL of 0.6 mg mL−1 FMNP dispersion. The Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 4.0) was chosen for the copper extraction of the wine samples because the pH 4 favors the reaction between copper and PAN. The Cu-FMNP complex was easily removed from samples by applying a magnetic field, which permitted fast and selective extraction/preconcentration (within 1 min) of the analyte with an enrichment factor of nine. After copper elution with FCD, FMNP alone can be reused up to two times without losing their adsorption efficiency. The FCD used in this work was synthesized from pineapple juice using hydrothermal carbonization and which is a low-cost, environmentally friendly, and non-toxic procedure. The fluorescence of the Cu-FCD complex is sensitively quenched in the presence of copper.

Comparison with previous analytical methods that require expensive instrumentations for acquisition, operation, and maintenance (as ETAAS, ICP–OES, USN-ICP-OES, TXRF, and SEC-ICP-MS) and use laborious and time-consuming sample pretreatment (as SEC, wet digestion, drying, and ashing), the proposed method employs a very inexpensive spectrofluorimeter easily found in routine laboratory and the analysis is carried out without sample pretreatment. In addition, instead of using a spectrofluorimeter, the fluorescence measurements can be performed using a cheaper handheld spectrometer (Ocean Optics, model USB4000, USA) as the detector, and a UV-LED (Lite-on, model LTPL-C034UVH370, China) as the excitation source at the 370 nm wavelength. It is worth emphasizing the pineapple is a fruit easily obtained on the market around the world and its cost is a few dollars and reagents used in the preparation of quantum carbon dots, magnetic nanoparticles, buffers, and acids, requiring cheap and common reagents available in most laboratories. Therefore, the total cost of each analysis using the proposed method can be roughly estimated to be at least ten times cheaper than the previous methods for copper determination.

The proposed method presents good intraday and interday RSD, LOD, and recovery values. The robustness of the proposed method was attested in the recovery and comparative studies with a reference method. Since this new method is fast, inexpensive, sensitive, selective, accurate, precise, and non-laborious for the determination of copper in wines at low concentration levels (<1.0 mg L–1) without sample pretreatment and risks to contamination and analyte loss, this new method can be a promising tool for the quality control of copper content in wine by regulatory agencies, wineries or commercials wine laboratories. So, if these laboratories want to reduce costs, the proposed method is a new, cheap and promising approach that can successfully replace traditional ETAAS, ICP–OES, USN-ICP-OES, TXRF, and SEC-ICP-MS methods for copper analysis. In addition, this new method becomes even more important in laboratories that have few financial resources for acquisition, operation, and maintenance of expensive as ETAAS, ICP–OES, USN-ICP-OES, TXRF, and SEC-ICP-MS instrumentations.

Matheus H. Costa: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis and Validation. Danilo T.S. Ferreira: Methodology, Formal analysis and Validation. Jonathan E.S. Pádua: Methodology, Formal analysis and Validation. Julys P.A. Fernandes: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation and Writing - original draft. Josué Carinhanha C. Santos: Resources, Supervision and Writing - original draft. Francisco Antônio S. Cunha: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision and Writing - original draft. Mario Cesar Ugulino Araujo: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financed in part by the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior (CAPES) under the Finance Code No. 001. The authors would like to thank the Brazilian agencies Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq) and CAPES for research fellowships and scholarships and research Grants N° 465768/2014-8 (INCTAA-CNPq). The English text of this paper has been revised by Sidney Pratt, Canadian, MAT (The Johns Hopkins University), RSAdip - TESL (Cambridge University).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preconcentration and determination of trace cadmium using 1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (pan) immobilized on surfactant-coated alumina
